front: What is a Moving Average (MA) process of order q?
back: A time series model, denoted as MA(q), where the current value is a linear combination of the mean, the current shock, and the past q shocks. Formally: X_t = μ + ε_t + θ_1ε_{t-1} + ... + θ_qε_{t-q}, where ε_t is white noise.

front: What are the key properties of an MA(1) process: X_t = μ + ε_t + θε_{t-1}?
back: 1) Mean E[X_t] = μ. 2) Variance: Var(X_t) = σ²_ε(1 + θ²). 3) Autocovariance: γ(1) = θσ²_ε, and γ(k)=0 for k≥2. 4) It is always (weakly) stationary.

front: What is the defining characteristic of the autocorrelation function (ACF) for an MA(q) process?
back: The AF has a sharp cutoff. It is non-zero only for lags up to the order q (i.e., for lags k = 1, 2, ..., q) and is zero for all lags k > q.

front: What is meant by the 'invertibility' of an MA process?
back: An MA process is invertible if it can be equivalently represented as an AR(∞) process. This requires the roots of the characteristic equation (1 + θ_1B + ... + θ_qB^q = 0) to lie outside the unit circle, ensuring a convergent series.

front: Why is the property of invertibility important for an MA model?
back: Invertibility ensures: 1) Uniqueness of the model (a given ACF corresponds to only one invertible MA process). 2) It provides a sensible, parsimonious interpretation where past observations have a diminishing effect on the present.
